canonical_definition: The prevailing usage across the provided literature frames "Argument quality" as a latent, multi-dimensional construct reflecting how cogent, reasonable, and well-structured an argument is. Several papers describe it as encompassing fine-grained dimensions such as clarity, credibility, logical sufficiency, coherence, relevance, and persuasiveness. One paper explicitly notes that it is a "multidimensional concept assessed through logical, rhetorical, and dialectical dimensions" ("Improving Large Language Models Function Calling and Interpretability via Guided-Structured Templates"). A less common framing, termed "Argumentative flow," defines the concept more narrowly as the ability to reproduce the "logical progression of claims, evidence, and rhetorical intent" in discourse ("Large Language Models Discriminate Against Speakers ofGerman Dialects"). To operationalize this construct, researchers use methods like "fine-grained argument quality evaluation," with one study assessing it "through the 15 fine-grained dimensions proposed by Wachsmuth et al. (2017b)" ("Waste Not, Want Not; RecycledGumbel Noise Improves Consistency in Natural Language Generation"). It is also measured through comparative judgments, where a "win-rate measures the proportion of instances where one argument is judged to be of higher quality than the other" ("MGM: Global Understanding of Audience Overlap Graphs for Predicting the Factuality and the Bias of News Media"). The `LLM-as-a-judge` evaluation paradigm is reported as one instrument used to measure argument quality.
papers: Improving Large Language Models Function Calling and Interpretability via Guided-Structured Templates
Large Language Models Discriminate Against Speakers ofGerman Dialects
Reward-Weighted Sampling: Enhancing Non-Autoregressive Characteristics in Masked DiffusionLLMs
MGM: Global Understanding of Audience Overlap Graphs for Predicting the Factuality and the Bias of News Media
Waste Not, Want Not; RecycledGumbel Noise Improves Consistency in Natural Language Generation

definitions.text: The latent trait reflecting how cogent, reasonable, and well-structured an argument is, encompassing multiple fine-grained dimensions such as clarity, credibility, and logical sufficiency.
definitions.paper: MGM: Global Understanding of Audience Overlap Graphs for Predicting the Factuality and the Bias of News Media

definitions.text: The latent ability of a model to capture and reproduce the logical progression of claims, evidence, and rhetorical intent in argumentative discourse, reflecting coherent and persuasive reasoning structure.
definitions.paper: Large Language Models Discriminate Against Speakers ofGerman Dialects

relationships.paper: MGM: Global Understanding of Audience Overlap Graphs for Predicting the Factuality and the Bias of News Media
